Statistics for Applications Course Notes
1 Course Overview
1.1 Goals
The primary objectives of this course are:
•To provide a solid introduction to the mathematical theory behind statistical meth-
ods
•To offer theoretical guarantees for the statistical methods used in various applica-
tions
1.2 Learning Outcomes
By the end of this class, you will be able to:
1. Formulate real-life situations as statistical problems in mathematical terms
2. Select appropriate statistical methods for your specific problem
3. Understand the implications and limitations of various statistical methods
2 Why Statistics?
Statistics has been essential across diverse fields throughout history. Understanding ran-
domness and making decisions under uncertainty are fundamental challenges that appear

domness and making decisions under uncertainty are fundamental challenges that appear
in numerous contexts.
2.1 Historical Applications
2.1.1 Hydrology (Netherlands, 10th century)
When building dams and dykes, engineers faced the following problem:
•Dams should be high enough to prevent most floods
•Dams should not be excessively expensive (i.e., not too high)
This requires understanding thedistributionof flood heights and making probabilistic
guarantees.
2.1.2 Insurance
Modern insurance relies heavily on statistical modeling:
•Given a person’s driving record, car information, and desired coverage
•What constitutes a fair premium?
4
Statistics for Applications Course Notes
2.1.3 Clinical Trials
Medical research depends on rigorous statistical analysis:
•A drug is tested on 100 patients

2.1.3 Clinical Trials
Medical research depends on rigorous statistical analysis:
•A drug is tested on 100 patients
•56 patients were cured, 44 showed no improvement
•Question: Is the drug effective, or could these results occur by chance?
2.2 The Common Thread: Randomness
Central Concept
All these examples share a fundamental characteristic:RANDOMNESS
Key questions arising from randomness include:
•Notion of average (“fair premium”, expected flood height, ...)
•Quantifying chance (“most of the floods”, probability of cure, ...)
•Assessing significance and variability
3 Probability vs Statistics
3.1 Probability: Working Forward
Definition 3.1(Probability).Probability studies randomness when the underlying phys-
ical process is completely known.

Definition 3.1(Probability).Probability studies randomness when the underlying phys-
ical process is completely known.
In some cases, the random mechanism is perfectly understood: dice, cards, roulette,
fair coins, etc.
Example
Example 1: Rolling 1 Die
Consider the following game:
•Alice gets 1€if the number of dots≥3
•Bob gets 2€if the number of dots≤2
•Question: Who do you want to be: Alice or Bob?
Analysis:
•Alice wins with probability4/6 = 2/3, expected value =1×2/3 = 0.67€
•Bob wins with probability2/6 = 1/3, expected value =2×1/3 = 0.67€
Both have the same expected value, so the game is fair!
5
Statistics for Applications Course Notes
Example
Example 2: Rolling 2 Dice
•Choose a number between 2 and 12
•Win 100€if you correctly predict the sum of the two dice

Example
Example 2: Rolling 2 Dice
•Choose a number between 2 and 12
•Win 100€if you correctly predict the sum of the two dice
•Which number should you choose?
Solution:The sum 7 has the highest probability (6/36), as it can be obtained in
the most ways: (1,6), (2,5), (3,4), (4,3), (5,2), (6,1).
Key Insight:In probability, we know the random process (e.g., 1/6 chance for each
face, dice are independent), and wededucethe probability of outcomes and expected
amounts.
3.2 Statistics: Working Backward
Statistics becomes necessary when dealing with more complicated processes where:
•We need toestimate parameters from data
•True randomness exists (random sampling, biased coins, measurement error)
•Phenomena are deterministic but too complex to model exactly (statistical model-
ing)

•Phenomena are deterministic but too complex to model exactly (statistical model-
ing)
Fundamental Modeling Principle
Complicated process = Simple process + Random noise
Good modeling consists of choosing a plausible simple process and an appropriate
noise distribution.
3.3 Contrasting Probability and Statistics
Example
Probability Perspective:
“Previous studies showed that the drug was 80% effective. Then we can anticipate
that for a study on 100 patients, on average 80 will be cured and at least 65 will
be cured with 99.99% probability.”
Statistics Perspective:
“We observe that 78/100 patients were cured. We conclude (with appropriate
methods) that we are 95% confident that for other studies the drug will be effective
on between 69.88% and 86.11% of patients.”
Key Distinction:

methods) that we are 95% confident that for other studies the drug will be effective
on between 69.88% and 86.11% of patients.”
Key Distinction:
•Probability:Known mechanism→Predict outcomes

•Statistics:Observed outcomes→Infer mechanism

4.6 Discussion of Assumptions
4.6.1 Assumption 1: Randomness
Randomness is a way of modeling lack of information. With perfect information about:
•The conditions of kissing
•What goes on in the kissers’ minds
•Physical positioning
Physics or sociology would theoretically allow us to predict the outcome determinis-
tically. Since we lack this information, we model the outcome as random.
4.6.2 Assumption 2: Bernoulli Distribution
SinceR i ∈ {0,1}, eachR i is necessarily a Bernoulli random variable.
More general model:We could assumeRi ∼Ber(p i)with different parameters for
each couple.
Problem:We don’t have enough information to estimate all thepi’s accurately.
Simplification:We assumep i =pfor alli(homogeneity assumption).

4.6.3 Assumption 3: Independence
The independence assumption is reasonable because:
•People were observed at different locations
•Observations occurred at different times
•There’s no obvious mechanism for dependence between couples

5.2.2 Key Insights and Interpretation
What the CLT Tells Us
1.Asymptotic Normality:Regardless of the original distribution ofX, the normal-
ized sample mean converges to a normal distribution. This is remarkable because
Xcould follow any distribution (exponential, uniform, Bernoulli, etc.) as long as
it has finite variance.

2.Rate of Convergence:The difference( ¯Xn −µ)decreases at rate1/ √n:
•To halve the error, you need 4 times as many samples
•To achieve 10×better accuracy, you need 100×more data
•The standard error isσ/ √n
3.Universal Result:The CLT is a universal phenomenon that works for ANY
distribution with finite variance. This universality is what makes it so powerful in
practice.

4.Foundation for Inference:Provides the theoretical basis for:
•Confidence intervals

5.2.6 Requirements and Conditions for the CLT
Essential Requirements
1.Independence:The random variablesX 1, . . . , Xn must be mutually independent
2.Identical Distribution:All variables must follow the same distribution

2.Identical Distribution:All variables must follow the same distribution
3.Finite Variance:The varianceσ 2 =Var[X]must be finite (i.e.,σ 2 <∞)
Remark 5.1.These conditions can be relaxed in more advanced versions of the CLT:

ditions

5.2.11 Common Misconceptions
Important Note
Misconception 1:"The CLT says the data becomes normally distributed."
Correction:The CLT says thesample mean(or sum) becomes approximately
normal. The individual data points retain their original distribution.
Misconception 2:"I needn≥30regardless of the distribution."
Correction:The rulen≥30is a rough guideline. The required sample size

Misconception 2:"I needn≥30regardless of the distribution."
Correction:The rulen≥30is a rough guideline. The required sample size
depends heavily on the original distribution’s shape.
Misconception 3:"The CLT applies to any statistic."
Correction:The classical CLT specifically applies to sample means. Other statis-
tics may or may not be asymptotically normal (though many are, under appropriate
conditions).
Misconception 4:"Largernalways means the CLT works better."
Correction:Whiletrueasymptotically, ifmodelassumptions(independence, iden-
tical distribution) are violated, increasingnwon’t fix the problem.
5.2.12 Summary: Why the CLT is Fundamental
The Central Limit Theorem is arguably the most important result in statistics because:

5.2.12 Summary: Why the CLT is Fundamental
The Central Limit Theorem is arguably the most important result in statistics because:
1.Universality:Works for any distribution with finite variance
2.Normality:Explains why normal distributions appear so frequently in practice
3.Practical inference:Provides the foundation for confidence intervals and hypoth-
esis tests
4.Rate quantification:Tells us how estimation error decreases with sample size
5.Asymptotictheory:Servesasthebuildingblockformoresophisticatedstatistical
methods
The CLT, together with the Law of Large Numbers, forms the mathematical founda-
tion for understanding why statistical inference works and provides quantitative tools for
practical data analysis.

10 Summary and Key Takeaways
10.1 Statistical Thinking Framework
The general approach to statistical problems:
1. Start with a real-world problem
2. Formulate a mathematical/statistical model with reasonable assumptions
3. Apply appropriate statistical methods (estimation, testing, etc.)

2. Formulate a mathematical/statistical model with reasonable assumptions
3. Apply appropriate statistical methods (estimation, testing, etc.)
4. Interpret results in the context of the original problem
5. Acknowledge limitations and assumptions
10.2 Fundamental Distinction: Probability vs. Statistics
Probability Statistics
Known mechanism Unknown mechanism
Predict outcomes Infer mechanism
Deductive reasoning Inductive reasoning
P[data|model] P[model|data]

1.Law of Large Numbers:
•Guarantees consistency of sample averages

10.6 Practical Considerations
Model assumptions matter:
•Independence assumptions are often unverifiable but necessary
•Distributional assumptions (e.g., exponential, Bernoulli) should be justified

Sample size considerations:

The problem with weak instruments
If instruments are too weakly correlated to theX’s, even if we increase the number of
observations, there can be an important bias in estimations
Plus, the estimator has a nonnormal sampling distribution which makes statistical
inference meaningless
The weak instrument problem is increased with many instruments, so drop the weakest
ones and use the most relevant ones
A way to measure how instruments are correlated to potentially endogenous variables is to
run the regression explaining the former by the latter and check its goodness of fit
A criterion can be the global F statistic: if F<10, then instruments are weak

Returns on education
Angrist-Krueger (1991), "Does Compulsory School Attendance Affect Schooling and Earnings",
Quarterly Journal of Economics
Goal: find causal link between education and wages, and we know that such a wage
equation is subject to endogeneity
In the US, compulsory education starts the year pupils turn 6, and ends when pupil turns 16
Pupils born in January: begin school at 6 years 8 months old
Pupils born in December: begin school at 5 years 9 months old
For pupils who stop school at 16, pupils born in December study almost one year more
than those born in January
Quarter of birth is thus correlated to schooling, but random so uncorrelated to individual
ability: good instrument for schooling

So we instrument the dummy indicating whether the individual was assigned to the
